answer:
In Allama Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, the poem "Bilad-e-Islamiya" evokes Baghdad as a *gulshan* symbolizing the lost splendor of an ideal Islamic polity—a vibrant, self-renewing community of prophetic successors whose power made empires tremble.[^1] The key couplet (in transliteration and facing-page translation) reads:

> Jis ke ghunche thay chaman saamaan, woh *gulshan* hai yahi  
> Kaanpta tha jin se Roma, un ka madfan hai yahi  
>   
> This is that garden whose every bud was a garden in itself;[^2]  
> This is the final tomb of those before whom Rome would tremble.[^3]

Here, Baghdad is no mere plot of land but a *gulshan* whose constituent elements (*ghunche*, or buds) are themselves *chaman* (gardens), figuring a fractal, infinitely generative ideal community under Islam's early caliphs. It rivals Iram (Qur'an 89:7, the paradigmatic lost paradise) as an earthly idyll of sacralized sovereignty and cultural efflorescence.[^4]

Rendering *gulshan* as plain "garden" understates its dense Persian-Urdu freight: not horticulture but a topos of eternal *bahaar* (spring), synesthetic harmony (rose-fragrance, nightingale-song, wine-cup), and erotic-spiritual enclosure—the beloved's realm in ghazal, Sufi *maqam* of divine proximity, or Timurid/Mughal utopia of ordered plenitude. English "garden" flattens this to botany; *gulshan* pulses with the *ishq* (passion) binding community in paradisial, anti-entropic renewal.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 2 "Bilad-e-Islamiya" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/transliteration/).
[^2]: /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-9bfa99.
[^3]: Ibid.
[^4]: /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-3ddf5a.
[^5]: Cf. *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, on nations as fruit-pickers or barren trees in "life’s great garden" (*gulshan-e-hasti*; /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-5b3059).
